---
title: "Predicting Survival Outcomes Using Logistic Regression"
#subtitle: "Summarizing Two Articles"
author: "Yafet Mekonnen (Advisor: Dr. Cohen)"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    date-format: long
course: Capstone Projects in Data Science
bibliography: references.bib # file contains bibtex for references
#always_allow_html: true # this allows to get PDF with HTML features
self-contained: true
execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---


## Introduction 

  Surviving or not, sick or cured many real life outcomes come down to a simple binary choice of “yes” or “no.” Logistic regression is one of the most common tools for predicting these kinds of outcomes. It can estimate who might survive an illness, who may be at risk for heart problems, or who is likely to develop diabetes. Researchers such as [@wang2024exploring], who analyzed heart disease risk, and [@joshi2021predicting] , who predicted type 2 diabetes, both show how powerful logistic regression can be when trying to understand which factors increase the chances of an event happening. Because it is a supervised learning method, logistic regression uses labeled data to learn patterns and predict the probability of an outcome making it a perfect approach for studying survival outcomes.
  
  Logistic regression will be used to examine which passenger features were most strongly linked to surviving one of the most tragic events in history, the sinking of the Titanic. Like any other machine learning model, the dataset must first be cleaned, missing values must be addressed, and meaningful predictors must be selected before any analysis can be done. As discussed in [@shipe2019developing], using clean data, properly handling missing information, and choosing the right predictors are essential steps for building a dependable model. After the Titanic dataset is fully prepared, the logistic regression model will evaluate relationships between passenger characteristics such as age, sex, ticket class, fare, and their overall chances of survival.
  
  The main goal of this paper is to build a model that predicts passenger survival in the Titanic shipwreck using logistic regression. This project will also focus on identifying which features are most important for making accurate predictions. Overall, the analysis will show how logistic regression can turn real life data into meaningful insights and help answer real world questions about why some passengers survived while others did not.
  
Disclaimer: Microsoft Copilot was used to assist with grammar while writing this introduction.
